---
title: "Moose abundance"
subtitle: |
  [NO_MOOS_001]
indicatorID: 
  - NO_MOOS_001
indicatorName: 
  - Moose abundance [Elgbestand]
verbatimName: 
  - Elgbestand
folderName: NO_MOOS_001
country: Norway
continent: Europe
ECT: B3 Functional state characteristics
Realm: 
  - Terrestrial (T) # e.g. Freshwater (F)
Biome: 
  - T2 Temperate-boreal forests and woodlands biome
Ecosystem: 
  - T2.1 Boreal/montane forests
yearAdded: 2026 # the year when the indicator was first submitted to ecRxiv
yearLastUpdate: 2026
status: incomplete # options are complete, incomplete, or deprecated
Version: "1.0.0" # See wiki page: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/wiki#versioning
VersionComment: First version on ecRxiv; indicator described in the Norwegian Nature Index
url: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_MOOS_001
Normalised: Yes
SpatialAggregationPathway: Aggregate (county level) - [Scale - Truncate] - Aggregate (national level)
format: 
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-depth: 3
    smooth-scroll: true
execute: 
  cache: true
author:
  - name: Aud H. Halbritter
    email: aud.halbritter@uib.no
    affiliations:
      - id: myID
        name: University of Bergen
AuthorList: Halbritter, A. H.
date: May 15, 2026
data_availability: gold
code_reproducibility: gold
open_science_badge: gold
callout-icon: false
lightbox: true
css: ../../../style.css
code-links:
      - text: Add a review
        icon: github
        href: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv
hide: FALSE # Change to FALSE
bibliography: references.bib
---

<!--# This is a template for how to document the indicator analyses. Make sure also to not change the order, or modify, the headers, unless you really need to. This is because it easier to read if all the indicators are presented using the same layout. If there is one header where you don't have anything to write, just leave the header as is, and don't write anything below it. If you are providing code, be careful to annotate and comment on every step in the analysis. Before starting it is recommended to fill in as much as you can in the metadata file. This file will populate the initial table in your output.-->

<!--# Load all you dependencies here -->

```{r setup}
#| include: false
#| cache: false
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(gt)
library(here)
library(anybadger)
library(yaml)
library(tibble)
library(conflicted)
# weird workarounds for cache issues
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::lag)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::select)
pull<-dplyr::pull
library(NIcalc)
library(ecTools)
library(MetBrewer)

# This workflow uses `renv`. Please run `renv::restore()` in order to reproduce the working environment and run the analysis.
# renv::restore()

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r source}
#| echo: false
#| cache: false
source(here::here("_common.R"))
```



<!--# The following parts are autogenerated. Do not edit. -->

::: {layout-ncol="6"}

```{r statusBadge}
#| echo: false
status_badge(as.character(st))
```

```{r versionBadge}
#| echo: false
version_badge(my_version_number = as.character(version[[1]]))
```

```{r dataBadge}
#| echo: false
data_badge(as.character(badges[[1]]))
```

```{r codeBadge}
#| echo: false
code_badge(as.character(badges[[2]]))
```

```{r openScienceBadge}
#| echo: false
open_science_badge(as.character(badges[[3]]))
```

```{r licenseBadge}
#| echo: false
# The following inserts a badge specifying the GPL GNU version 3 license. ecRxiv recommends using this license, but you can decide to use another. Contact ecRxiv for help in insert a badge for a different license
license_badge()
```


::: 
 

> **Recommended citation**: `r paste(auth, " ", year, ". ", name, " (ID(s): ", folder_name, ") ", "v. ", version, ". ecRxiv: ", url, sep="")`



::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Metadata
```{r tbl-meta2}
#| tbl-cap: 'Indicator metadata'
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
meta |>
  dplyr::filter(Variable %in% c(
    "Indicator ID",
    "Indicator Name",
    "Continent",
    "Country",
    "Ecosystem Condition Typology Class", 
    "Realm",
    "Biome",
    "Ecosystem",
    "Year added", 
    "Last update",
    "Version",
    "Version comment",
    "Normalised",
    "Spatial aggregation pathway")) |>   
  kable()

```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}

## Log

<!--# Update this logg with short messages for each update -->
- May 15, 2026 - Initial draft for ecRxiv
:::


<hr />

<!--# Document you work below.  -->

## 1. Summary

<!--# 
With a maximum of 300 words, describe the indicator in general terms as you would to a non-expert. Think of this as a kind of common language summary. Follow the four numbers sub-headers below. under point 2, it is possible under  include a bullet point list of the specific steps in the workflow. 
 -->

1. **Background and rationale**:
The moose (_Alces alces_) is a large cervid associated with forest ecosystems in Norway.
In the period with reasonably good overview of cervid densities in Norway (about the last 200 years), populations have been strongly influenced by human activity, and in several areas current moose densities are considered higher than the reference condition.

2. **Methods**:
The moose abundance indicator (elgbestand) expresses population density as number of individuals per km² below the climatic tree line, reported at county (_fylke_) level, as described on [Naturindeksen – elg](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/elg).
Underlying population information for moose in Norway is linked to the national monitoring programme for cervids (elg, hjort, rådyr, and villrein), which has operated since 1991 and is owned by Landbruksdirektoratet and run by NINA [@hjortevilt_nasjonal_overvaking].
Much of the monitoring material is collected from animals harvested during hunting (for example jaw bones, slaughter weight, and for moose also reproductive organs from females) [@hjortevilt_nasjonal_overvaking].
The reference condition is an expert-assessed state in which moose may occur throughout suitable areas under current climate and predator regimes, but where densities are kept low enough through hunting that populations do not substantially harm other biodiversity (for example through intense browsing).
For Naturindeks, estimated abundance or equivalent indices by county are normalised using a unimodal scaling function.
Scaled county values are aggregated to five geographic regions and national level.

3. **Interpretation**:
An indicator value of 1 means that moose abundance in the reporting unit is at least as large as expected under the reference condition used in Naturindeksen.
Values decreasing toward zero indicate that the population index is further below that reference.

4. **Results and development needs**:



## 1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

1. **Bakgrunn og begrunnelse**: Elg (_Alces alces_) er et stort hjortedyr knyttet til skogøkosystemer i Norge. I perioden med rimelig oversikt over hjortevilttettheter i Norge (ca. 200 år) har bestandene vært sterkt påvirket av menneskelig aktivitet, og i flere områder vurderes dagens elgtettheter som høyere enn referansetilstanden. Data leveres på fylkesnivå.

2. **Metoder**: Indikatoren for elgbestand uttrykker tetthet som antall individer per km² under den klimatiske tregrensen, rapportert på fylkesnivå, som beskrevet på [Naturindeksen – elg](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/elg). Underliggende bestandsinformasjon er knyttet til det nasjonale overvåkingsprogrammet for hjortevilt [@hjortevilt_nasjonal_overvaking], med mye materiale fra jakt [@hjortevilt_nasjonal_overvaking] og statistikk via [Hjorteviltregisteret](https://www.hjorteviltregisteret.no) [@hjorteviltregisteret]. Referansetilstanden er en ekspertvurdert tilstand der elg kan forekomme i egnete områder under dagens klima og rovdyrregimer, men der tettheten holdes lav nok gjennom jakt til at bestanden ikke i vesentlig grad skader annet biologisk mangfold. For Naturindeks normaliseres fylkesverdier med en unimodals skaleringsfunksjon. Skalerte fylkesverdier aggregeres til fem geografiske regioner og nasjonalt nivå.
 
3. **Tolkning**: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at elgbestanden i rapporteringsenheten er minst like stor som forventet under referansetilstanden. Verdier mot null viser at bestandsindekset ligger lenger under referansen.

4. **Resultater og utviklingsbehov**:


## 2. About the underlying data

The moose abundnace data comes from the national cervid monitoring programme and Hjorteviltregisteret [@hjortevilt_nasjonal_overvaking; @hjorteviltregisteret].

### 2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Indicator values are available for the assessment years in Naturindeksen: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024.

To produce indicator estimates for these years, input is typically combined over a short window (typically two to three years), as specified in the Naturindeks indicator protocol.

Spatially, data and indicator values are handled at county (_fylke_) scale as IC units in Naturindeksen, then aggregated to five geographic regions and national level for reporting.

### 2.2 Sampling design

The national monitoring programme for cervids collects most material from harvested animals during hunting seasons [@hjortevilt_nasjonal_overvaking].
For moose, samples can include lower jaw and slaughter weight, and reproductive organs (egg stalks) from females [@hjortevilt_nasjonal_overvaking].
Further methodological detail for how county abundance enters Naturindeks is given on [Naturindeksen – elg](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/elg).

### 2.3 Original units

The moose abundance is expressed as number of individuals per km².

### 2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

Monitoring programme: [@hjortevilt_nasjonal_overvaking] and [Hjorteviltregisteret](https://www.hjorteviltregisteret.no) [@hjorteviltregisteret].

Methodological documentation: [Naturindeksen – elg](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/elg).

<!-- ### 2.5 Additional comments about the dataset -->

<!--# Optional. Text here -->

## 3. Indicator properties

### 3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

<!--# Describe the rationale for assigning the indicator to the ECT class. See https://oneecosystem.pensoft.net/article/58218/
This doesnt need to be very long. Maybe just a single sentence. -->

B3 Functional state characteristics.

<!-- 
### 3.1.1 Other condition typologies -->

<!--# Optional: Add text about other standards or typologies, such as alternative national condition typologies, and how the indicator relates to these -->


### 3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

<!--# 

Describe the ecosystem condition characteristic represented in the indicator. See 10.3897/oneeco.6.e58218 for information on what these characteristics might be.
For example, and indicator called 'Trenching in mires' could be made to represent an ecosystem characteristic 'Intact hydrology'. The term 'characteristic' is used similar to the term 'criteria' in Multiple Criteria Decision Making.  

-->


### 3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

<!--# Describe known collinearities with other metrics (indicators or variables) that could become problematic if they were also included in the same Ecosystem Condition Assessment as the indicator described here. -->

The indicator may covary with other cervid abundance indicators (red deer, roe deer) and with other species indicators that share similar habitats or data sources.

### 3.4 Impact factors

<!--# Describe the main natural and anthropogenic factors that affecst the metric -->


### 3.5 Spatial aggregation

Following the metadata pathway Aggregate (population model, county level) – [Scale – Truncate] – Aggregate (national level): population estimates or indices are available per county (IC unit in Naturindeksen).
Each county value is normalised with a unimodal scaling function (optimum at _X~100~_; lower and upper reference values at _X~0~_; see Section 4.2).
Regional (east, south, west, central, north) and national indicator values are then obtained as area-weighted combinations of the scaled county values.

## 4. Reference condition and levels

### 4.1 Reference condition

The species-specific reference condition for moose is ...

### 4.2 Reference levels

For this indicator, normalisation uses a unimodal scaling function.
Optimum abundance corresponds to _X~100~_.
The lower and upper reference values on the variable scale are both set at _X~0~_, so that densities both below and above the optimum are scaled towards worst condition.

#### 4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

Reference levels and indicator values apply at county (_fylke_) scale (IC units).
Aggregated results for the five Naturindeks geographic regions and for Norway are summaries of those units with area weights.
Normalisation should be done at county scale before regional or national aggregation.

## 5. Uncertainties
### 5.1 General uncertainties

Reference levels are held fixed in this workflow (their own uncertainties are not propagated).
Regional and national values are area-weighted summaries of counties, so they inherit both model uncertainty at county scale and any mismatch between county boundaries and ecological variation within regions.

### 5.2 Reported uncertainties

The intervals in @tbl-moos-results and @fig-moos-timeSeries are **inferential**: they describe uncertainty around the estimated area-weighted mean indicator for each reporting unit (a geographic region or Norway as a whole), given the statistical models used for county-level abundance in Naturindeksen.

For each county and assessment year, Naturindeks provides a predictive distribution for abundance (expressed on the variable scale before scaling).
We draw many random values from that distribution per county, scale each draw to the 0–1 indicator scale using the county reference optimum and unimodal scaling, then build a large set of possible county indicators.
To obtain uncertainty for a region, we repeat the following 1000 times: draw one random county indicator per county in that region, take the forest-area-weighted average across those counties, and record that regional mean.
The median of those regional means is reported as the point estimate; the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles form an approximate 95% interval, and the 25% and 75% quantiles form an approximate interquartile range.
For Norway, we apply a second Monte Carlo step at regional scale: draw one value from each region’s uncertainty distribution, take the forest-area-weighted mean across the five regions, and repeat 1000 times to obtain a national distribution, then summarise in the same way.
This approach reflects uncertainty in the county models and in the scaled values, while treating the regional or national figure as a single summary parameter rather than as a random draw from one county.


## 6. References

::: {#refs}
:::

## 7. Import and prepare datasets

Run the download chunk once to refresh the local export. This project’s renv environment installs `NIcalc` from GitHub `ChloeRN/NIcalc`, branch `include_ReadOnly_access` (see `renv.lock` and `install_deps.R`); that build defaults to the Naturindeks read-only API user, so you do not need `username` / `password` arguments here. When [NINAnor/NIcalc PR #59](https://github.com/NINAnor/NIcalc/pull/59) is merged into `NINAnor/NIcalc`, you can switch the renv install back to the main repo and update the lockfile.
We save the RDS export under `data/` so that we do not need to download the data every time we run the analysis.

```{r moos-import-api}
#| code-summary: "Download moose data from Naturindeks API"
#| eval: false

moosImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Elg"),
  year = c("1990", "2000", "2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)

saveRDS(moosImport, here::here("data/moosImport.rds"))
```

```{r moos-load-import}
#| include: false
#| cache: false
#| code-summary: "Load saved Naturindeks import (enable when data/moosImport.rds exists)"

moosImport <- readRDS(here::here("data/moosImport.rds"))
```


## 8. Spatial units

Accoring to the Naturindeks terminology, the Norwegian counties are BSunits, the 15 counties are IC units, and the five geographic regions in Norway (east, south, west, central, north) are NI units. 
We can think of the counties as ecosystem assets, and mainland Norway as the Ecosystem accounting Area.

## 9. Analyses

This section uses the following workflow: we take variable values and reference levels from the Naturindeks import, normalise at county scale, then propagate uncertainty and aggregate spatially with area weights using `ecTools::ec_upscale()`.
At regional and national scale, uncertainties are inferential: each replicate is an area-weighted mean based on one draw per county (then, for Norway, one draw per region).
We use unimodal scaling and forest area (`Skog`) as weights.

County-level abundance in the database is stored as a predictive distribution (parameters or custom distributions), not only as a point estimate.
Reference optimum density per county comes from the reference slice of the same download (`expectedValue` for the reference year).
We ignore uncertainty in reference levels.

### Assemble import and indicator object

`assembleNiObject()` reshapes `moosImport` so that indicator observations are in one list element per assessment year, and so that municipality-level `BSunits` (with forest area and NI region code `part`) are aligned with the thematic index structure used in Naturindeks.

```{r moos-assemble}
#| code-summary: "Assemble moose NI object (thematic, predefined regions)"
moosAssemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = moosImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)
```

### Reference levels (X~100~)

Before scaling, we need the county-specific optimum on the variable scale (moose density, ind. km⁻² below the tree line).
These are read from `referenceValues` in the RDS export (rows with `yearId == 0` or reference year name), one `x100` per IC unit (county).

```{r moos-refs}
#| code-summary: "County reference optimum (X100) from downloaded Naturindeks data"
x100 <- moosImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
  dplyr::filter(.data$yearId == 0L | grepl("Referanse", .data$yearName, fixed = FALSE)) |>
  dplyr::distinct(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue)
x100 |>
  gt::gt()
```

### Resample non-scaled county-scale variables

For each assessment year, Naturindeks stores county values as a parameterised distribution (or a custom distribution).
We draw `nsim = 1000` random values per county with `NIcalc::sampleObsMat()`.
Rows with a standard distribution family use observation type `tradObs`; rows with only a custom distribution use `customObs`.

Assessment years are the following: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024.
The result `dat` is a long table of simulated variable values (`value`) by `ICunitId` and `year` (not yet on the 0–1 indicator scale).

```{r moos-vars}
#| code-summary: "Observation types for sampleObsMat"
myYears <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- vector("list", length(myYears))
for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  obs <- moosAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName
  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"
  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}
```

```{r moos-draw}
#| code-summary: "Resample county-scale variables (non-scaled)"
#| warning: false
#| message: false
dat <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = moosAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = moosAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = moosAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = moosAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = moosAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = moosAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  colnames(myMat) <- paste0("s", seq_len(ncol(myMat)))
  myMat <- myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId"), names_to = "sim_col", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(
      sim_id = readr::parse_number(.data$sim_col),
      year = myYears[i]
    ) |>
    select(ICunitId, sim_id, value, year)

  dat <- dat |>
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(dat) |>
  gt::gt()
```

### Scaling to indicator values (unimodal)

Normalisation is done at county scale before any regional or national aggregation.
Each draw is joined to its county `x100`, then mapped to `[0, 1]` with `ecTools::ec_normalise()` in two-sided mode: `x0 = 0` and `x100` = optimum from the download define the best condition; `x0h = 2 * x100` is a simple upper anchor for “too high” density (adjust if you adopt explicit upper limits from NI documentation).
The `sigmoid` option applies a non-linear transform after the piecewise linear mapping, in line with unimodal indicators in Naturindeks [@nater_naturindeks_2025].

```{r moos-join-scale}
#| code-summary: "Join X100 and unimodal scaling to indicator scale"
dat <- dat |>
  left_join(x100 |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)), by = join_by(ICunitId))

ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = dat$value,
  x0 = 0,
  x100 = dat$x100,
  x0h = 2 * dat$x100,
  fun = "sigmoid"
)

dat <- dat |> add_column(indicator = ind)
```

The scatter plot below (@fig-moos-norm) shows the mapping from variable to indicator for each county (one facet per `ICunitName`). The figure is saved when the document is rendered.

```{r moos-normplot}
#| eval: true
#| code-summary: "Save normalisation diagnostic figure (English)"
moose_plot <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ dat$ICunitName)
ggsave(filename = here::here("img/moos_norm_plot_eng.png"), plot = moose_plot, scale = 1.5)
```

```{r moos-fig-norm}
#| label: fig-moos-norm
#| fig-cap: "Unimodal normalisation of moose density to indicator values (one panel per county)."
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/moos_norm_plot_eng.png"))
```

### Aggregation weights (forest area by county)

Spatial aggregation uses area below the tree line recorded in Naturindeks (`Skog` on each municipality in `BSunits`).
Municipalities are linked to counties via `ICunits`, then forest area is summed to county (`ICunitId`) and the NI region `part` (N, C, W, S, E) is kept for the next step.
Each row in `dat` then carries `forest_area` and `part` for weighting.

```{r moos-weights}
#| code-summary: "Forest area by county and NI region for aggregation weights"
aux <- as_tibble(moosAssemble$BSunits) |>
  rename(BSunitId = id)

IC_BC_link <- moosImport$ICunits |>
  select(ICunitId = id, BSunitId)

aux <- aux |>
  left_join(IC_BC_link, by = join_by(BSunitId))

aux <- aux |>
  group_by(ICunitId) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(Skog), part = unique(part)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))

dat <- dat |>
  left_join(aux, by = join_by(ICunitId))
```

### Spatial aggregation

Aggregate from counties to geographic regions with `ecTools::ec_upscale()`, using Monte Carlo propagation of inferential uncertainty (one draw per county per replicate, forest-area-weighted mean).

```{r moos-aggregate1}
#| code-summary: "County to region MC propagation (ec_upscale)"
#| eval: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
mc_propagated <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = dat,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = forest_area,
  start_units = ICunitId,
  end_units = part,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated, here::here("data/moos_mc_propagated.rds"))
```

```{r moos-load-mc-regional}
#| include: false
mc_propagated <- readRDS(here::here("data/moos_mc_propagated.rds"))
head(mc_propagated)
```

For the national indicator, propagate again from regions to Norway.
Summed forest area per geographic region is used as weight at this step.

```{r moos-aux2}
#| code-summary: "Total forest area per NI region"
aux2 <- aux |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(.data$forest_area), .groups = "drop")
aux2
```

```{r moos-agg-national}
#| code-summary: "Region to Norway MC propagation (ec_upscale)"
#| eval: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
mc_propagated <- mc_propagated |>
  left_join(aux2, by = join_by(part)) |>
  add_column(end_units = "Norway")

mc_propagated2 <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = mc_propagated,
  variable = sampled_mean,
  weight = forest_area,
  start_units = part,
  end_units = end_units,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated2, here::here("data/moos_mc_propagated2.rds"))
```

```{r moos-load-mc-national}
#| include: false
mc_propagated2 <- readRDS(here::here("data/moos_mc_propagated2.rds"))
head(mc_propagated2)
```

Regional and national MC draws are combined for summarisation and export.

```{r moos-mc-combined}
#| code-summary: "Bind regional and national MC outputs"
mc_propagated3 <- mc_propagated |>
  select(year, part, sampled_mean) |>
  bind_rows(mc_propagated2)
```

## 10. Results

From the 1000 Monte Carlo replicates per region and year in `mc_propagated3`, we report the median as the point estimate and use the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval for the area-weighted mean indicator (inferential uncertainty, not descriptive between-county spread).
The table (@tbl-moos-results) and time series (@fig-moos-timeSeries) summarise regional and national (`Norway`) values.

```{r moos-part-labels}
#| include: false
moos_part_labels <- c(
  N = "North",
  C = "Central",
  W = "West",
  S = "South",
  E = "East",
  Norway = "Norway",
  All = "Norway"
)
moos_region_levels <- c("Norway", "Central", "East", "North", "South", "West")
```

```{r moos-summaryStats}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| cache: false
#| tbl-cap: "Moose indicator summaries by region and year (inferential uncertainty for area-weighted means)."
summary_out <- mc_propagated3 |>
  mutate(part = dplyr::recode(part, !!!moos_part_labels)) |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(sampled_mean), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.025), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.25), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.75), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.975), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(part = factor(part, levels = moos_region_levels)) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B3",
    Indicator = "Moose abundance",
    Ecosystem = "Boreal/montane forests",
    ID = "NO_MOOS_001"
  )
```

```{r moos-fig-timeSeries}
#| label: fig-moos-timeSeries
#| cache: false
#| fig-cap: "Indicator values for moose abundance by region and nationally, with uncertainty bands from the resampled distribution."
moos_plot_years <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
moos_region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(moos_region_levels)),
  moos_region_levels
)

plot_data <- summary_out |>
  mutate(
    year = factor(year, levels = moos_plot_years),
    series_type = if_else(part == "Norway", "National", "Region")
  )

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part, group = part)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.65,
    linewidth = 0.5,
    fatten = 3,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = moos_region_cols, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(National = 19, Region = 17),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c(National = 1, Region = 0.55),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)),
    shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 1.1, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")
```

```{r moos-tbl-results}
#| label: tbl-moos-results
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| cache: false
#| tbl-cap: "Indicator values and uncertainty estimates for moose abundance."

summary_out |>
  arrange(part, year) |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()
```

## 11. Export file

```{r export}
#| code-summary: "Export MC-propagated indicator draws"
#| eval: false
write.csv(
  mc_propagated3,
  here::here("data/results_NO_MOOS_001_v1.0.0.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)
```


<!-- Fill in CReDIT and MeRIT statments to clarify the contributions from individual authors. For MeRIT, see https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-37039-1. For CRedit, see https://credit.niso.org/. You can start with the template structur below, and just delete the rows you dont use.  -->

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on _Naturindeks_ [@nater_naturindeks_2025].

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Session Info
<!-- You can leave this last part as it is, unless you have a good reason to alter it. -->

This information is mainly aimed at developers that need to recreate the R environment that produced the html.
Important note: this information is about the R session that was running when the html was rendered, and if some of the code chunks were not evaluated when the html was rendered, they may have been executed in a different environment.  

```{r sessionInfo}
#| echo: false
sessionInfo()
```

:::
